segments.text: Then another Suta, three point, three point, five point, 44, the Buddha said, in three respects
monks, a Tal is profitable, what three, when he who teaches Dhamma and he who listens and both
alike are able to penetrate both the spirit and the letter thereof, that's the end of the
Suta.
So, here the Buddha is saying a Tal, that means a Dhamma Tal would be very profitable.
If the teacher, the one who speaks the Dhamma understands the Dhamma and the one who listens
is also able to understand and both are able to penetrate the spirit and the letter of
the Dhamma and there would be very, very good.
Next Suta, a three point, five point, 45, the Buddha said, monks, these three things
have been instructed by the wise and good, what three, first one is charity, second one, support
of mother and father, third one, going forth, that means renouncing.
These are the three duties, that's the end of the Suta.
So, the Buddha is saying, why is people always at wise people to do these three things?
The first one is to practice giving, giving means giving to those who need, who need, who
are in need.
One thing I like to mention is that the Buddha says that if we do charity, when we give,
we should not look for the merit, if we look for the merit, then there is a selfish attitude,
then your merit will become much less and it is observed sometimes, you know, for example,
people, they think, some lay people, they think, giving to a virtuous monk is very good.
So they like to go, I look for famous monks to give to, and sometimes famous monks don't
need the data, the offerings because they have so much, they are overflowing with offerings.
Whereas sometimes I might be a young monk, a newly renounce monk, and I know in monk who
needs support, and if you have the wisdom to go and offer where it is needed, and that
would be even more meritorious because you don't have a selfish intention, you do data because it's
good to do, you think that it is necessary to do, for example, to support the Sarsana, to support
the Sangha, whoever, whichever monk is need, and you do. Sometimes we find that there are sometimes
some temples, can be have too much offerings, you know, sometimes like in certain Buddhist countries
like Thailand, and there are people who come in, it's even written some books, that perhaps in
a certain area, there's a need for hospital, but people are not interested to build a hospital,
it won't give the offerings to the temple, which sometimes shows, it's the degree for
getting merit. The second one, support of mother and father, support of mother and father,
it's not sending mother and father to all homes. Nowadays, we find a lot of people sending
their old parents to all books home, support of mother and father means to look after them at home,
because I'm sure, every one of us, when we are old, we don't like our children to send us to the
old books home. We like to at least be around the children and the grandchildren and die at home.
So, whatever you like for yourself, I'm sure other people have the same idea, so it is
have to remember the love and the care that we owe to our parents. The third one is about
going forth, going forth is also advised by wise people, if you have done your family duties,
it is good to go forth, it is a kind of Indian tradition, you know, of Indians, even if they
don't go forth, the other certain age, they practice at home, they have a keep a room to themselves,
where they meditate, where they see their prayers, and so these are the three things, which are quite good.
Now, the next two types, three point five point forty seven, the Buddha said, monks,
there are these three marks of that which is conditioned, some kata, the parivati, some kata,
conditioned. What? 3. It's birth is a parent, it's passing away is a parent. It's changed ability
while it persises its a parent. These are the three marks of the conditioned, monks, there are these
three marks of the uncondition. What? 3? Its birth is not a parent, it's passing away,
is not a parent, it's changed ability while it persises, it's not a parent. These are the three
mouse of the uncondition. So in this uta, we are taller than anything that is
conditioned has these three characteristics. It has a bird, it has a passing
away or death, and it changes while it is still the next uta is 3.6.56. A certain
Brahmin upgrade, well, came to see the exalted one, then paid respect, etc. As he
said, to one side, he said this to the exalted one. Must go tamar. I have heard the
saying of Brahmins of all, ordained days, men aged far gone in years, themselves the
teachers of teachers. That once upon a time, this world was intruded with people. One might
think it to be the waveless deep. That religious suburb and royal cities were close enough
for a cockerel to walk from one to another. Pray, must go tamar. What is the reason? What is the
cause of the apparent loss and decrease of human beings? How is it that religious are no longer
villages, suburbs, no longer suburbs, towns, no longer towns, and districts are depopulated
then the putary plight? Just now Brahmin, people are blaze with unlawful lusts, overwhelmed by
depraved longings, obsessed by wrong doctrines, thus ablaze overwhelmed and obsessed. They see
sharp knives and take each other's lives, thus many men come by the end. That Brahmin is the reason
why these things are so. Again, Brahmin, since folk are ablaze with unlawful lusts, overwhelmed
by depraved longings, obsessed by wrong doctrines, on such as these the sky raised not down steadily.
It is hard to get a meal. The crops are abad, afflicted with new dew and grown to mere
stubs. Accordingly, many come to the end. That Brahmin is the reason. Again, Brahmin, since folk are
ablaze with unlawful lusts, etc., obsessed by wrong doctrines, on such misfrians, the yarkas,
blues, non-human monsters, thereby many come by the end. This Brahmin is the reason. This is the cause
of the apparent loss and decrease of human beings. That is why villages are no longer villages,
suburbs, no longer suburbs, down no longer towns and districts are depopulated.
And the Brahmin said, excellent master-goatama, excellent master-goatama,
made the worthy-goatama accept me as a lay-follower from this day for as long as life lasts,
as one who has gone to him for refuge. And this is an interesting suitah. It tells us now that
long time ago the world was populated with so many people that cities are near, so near each other,
that a cockroach could walk from one to another city. And the place is overflowing with people,
you know? But nowadays this must less. And the reason for this, according to the Buddha,
as a few reason, the first one is because of greed, hatred and delusion, people go to war.
And Qilis are there. And then the second reason is because of greed, hatred and delusion,
and falling wrong doctrines, the devas, the notice, you may remember, there's now the sutah,
that about four times a month, this devas come and observe us. And because they see that
the greed, hatred and delusion of human beings are too great. At this top, the range from coming down,
it seems that reins are also controlled by them. So because the reins don't come at the right time,
the crops don't grow well, so people starve to death. And the third one is when greed hatred
and delusion is too great among human beings. Then these higher devas, they let loose very fears,
the occurs, and these fears, the occurs can come and kill human beings. And you know sometimes
that some people are possessed by spirits, and that these are some of the spirits probably.
So why people can have too much greed, hatred and delusion, partly because they follow wrong doctrines,
and also the leaders have a lot to do with it. It's leaders of whether they are
male persons or monks also have a part to play. If leaders are corrupt, then it is very easy for
followers also to be corrupt, but if they have good leaders, then people will also imitate.
And the next two ties, three points, six points, fifty-seven.
Vachagota, the wonderer, came to see the exalted one and paid respect, etc. Then as he said to
one side, Vachagota, the wonderer said this to the exalted one. Have heard it said, Master Gautama,
that Gautama, the recluse, speaks to us. Arms should be given to me, not to others.
Two my followers, not to another's followers. Arms given to me are of great fruit,
not so on those given to others. Arms given to my followers are of great profit,
not arms given to another's followers. Now Master Gautama, do they who say these things correctly
repeat the views of the worthy Gautama without misrepresenting him by uttering and untruth?
Do they expound their views in accordance with his teaching so that one who is of his doctrine
of his way of thinking may not give grounds for reproach in stating it? Indeed, we are anxious not
to misrepresent the worthy Gautama, and the putary plight. Those who say so, Vachag, are not of my way
of thinking. Moreover, such misrepresent me by stating what is not true, but a falsehood. Indeed,
Vachag, who so prevents another from giving arms, obstructs him in three ways,
robs him of three things? What three? First, he obstructs the giver in acquiring merit.
Second, he prevents the receivers from getting a given. The, his own self is already ruined,
utterly ruined. Who so prevents another from giving arms, Vachag, obstructs him in three ways,
robs him of three things? But this is what I really do declare, Vachag, if one should throw away
spot, spot scarring, or the rinsings of cups into a pool, or says pit, that means a pit of
experiment. Even with the idea of feeding the creatures that live there in, I declare it would be
a source of merit to him to say nothing of his feeding beings that are human. Nevertheless, I say
Vachag, that a given in the case of the virtuous is of great truth, not those given in the
case of the wicked. By virtuous, I mean one who has abandoned five qualities and possesses five
qualities. What are the five qualities he has abandoned, sensual desire is abandoned,
malevolent, sloth and topper, excitement and glory, doubt and wavering are abandoned. These are
the five and of what five qualities is he possessed. He possesses the constituents of morality,
possessed by the adept, the constituents of concentration, wisdom, liberation, knowledge and vision
of liberation. He has these five qualities thus, where five qualities are abandoned and five possessed,
a gift is of great truth, I declare. This is interesting subtah, because here, somebody is asking
the Buddha whether he actually said that offerings should be made to him and his followers, not to
at the other external sac followers, and the Buddha denied it. The Buddha said, he never said that
and I said, anybody says such a thing, then in three ways he has done great wrong and the Buddha also
says that even the crumbs on the left over of food, even you throw into a pool, or a bit of
excitement with the idea of feeding the creatures there, even then also you get some merit not to
talk about feeding human beings, and then lastly the Buddha says that if you give to an harahan,
one who has abandoned the five hindrances and possessed of five other qualities of the harahan,
then you would have great merit. Now one thing I like to remark here is that one of the wrong
livelihoods for one of the wrong ways of livelihoods for a monk is to be little another monk personally
to talk bad and about another monk and discourage lay people from making offerings to another monk.
That is very bad, because here you can see that the Buddha is saying even those external sac people,
even then also he does not see that you should not make offerings to them. Even external
sac people even with wrong views and the Buddha, in fact, it is one sutra where the followers
of a gene sac had it. We can't ask after the left and the cantas and came to follow the Buddha,
the Buddha still advised them to continue giving to those negantas, those are genes, monks and so
so you can see that anyone practicing the spiritual path, we should not be little others,
even though they are, I mean not to the extent of asking people not to make offerings to these
other people. The 3.6.58, now the cantas, the Brahmin came to visit the exalted one,
on coming to him he greeted him, Peter's back, etc. Then he said to one side and the Brahmin sang
the praises of those Brahmin's who possess the threefold knowledge which is in Pali's TV ja.
Then the exalted one said the Buddha said, yes Brahmin, they have the threefold knowledge,
those Brahmin's, they have it as you say. But tell me how Brahmin's described Brahmin's who have
the threefold knowledge. And he can't ask it, in this case Master Gautama, a Brahmin is well-born
on both sides of pure descent from mother and father as far back as seven generations,
unsmurched without reproach in respect of birth. Number two, one given to study one who knows
the mantra by heart, a master in the three Vedas that indexes together with the ritual,
the treatise on chronology and the legendary saints, he is a man learned in the idioms and the
grammar and the third, one perfect in the science of world speculation and the science of the great
being. That Master Gautama is how the Brahmin's described a Brahmin who has the threefold knowledge.
I'll stop here just to recapitulate the Buddha. The Brahmin said that three qualities which a Brahmin
would be respected for. One first one is a pure Brahmin for seven generations and for seven generations
at least, at least, he is born on the mother side and father side now from pure Brahmin's.
Second one, he is very learned in the mantra in the Vedas, etc. Third one, he is knowledgeable on
world speculation, the science of world speculations and the science of the great being.
Then the Buddha said, well Brahmin, this description of a Brahmin who has the threefold knowledge
is one thing. The description of him who has the threefold knowledge in the discipline of the
Aryan is quite another thing. Then he can ask, but how Master Gautama is one the possessor of the threefold
knowledge in the discipline of the Aryan? Well for me, if the word he gotama would teach me according to
one method, he is so possessed and Buddha said, then Brahmin do you listen, apply your mind
attentively, I will speak. Very well Master replied to Kana the Brahmin to the exalted one and the
exalted one said, during among a lieu from sensuality, a lieu from evil states enters on the
first genre, which is accompanied by thought, directed and sustained born of solitude, delightful
and pleasurable, abides therein. Then by the calming down of thought, directed and sustained,
he enters on that inward calm that one pointlessness of mind, apart from thought, directed and
sustained, that is born of concentration, delightful and pleasurable, which is the second genre
and abides therein. Then by the fading out of delight, he abides,
equanimous, mindful and composed, and experiences pleasures through the body. Having entered
on the third genre, which the Aryans described in these terms, he is equanimous and mindful,
he who is equanimous and mindful, dwells happily, he abides therein. Then by the abandoning of
pleasure, by the abandoning of this comfort, by the ending of the happiness and unhappiness that
he had before entering on that state, which is neither pleasant nor painful, that utter purity
of mindfulness reached by equanimity, which is the both genre, he abides therein. Just stop here
for a while to comment on these genres, these genres are states of deep tranquility, where the
mind is very peaceful, becomes one-pointed, and some people, they don't understand the genres,
they belong to the genres, and they say that when you are in genre, you don't have any mindfulness,
but we notice here, in the fourth genre, the Buddha says that there is the utter purity of mindfulness,
the utter purity, I suppose, is to the complete purity of mindfulness, so you definitely cannot
say that a person in genre is not mindful, he is definitely mindful. In fact, the more concentrated
that person is, the stronger, his mindfulness is, but he is directed on one thing,
so there's this something I like to mention, then the Buddha continued, he then with mind,
thus composed, made pure and translucent, stainless, and with his gains vanished away, made
flying and workable, fixed and unperturbed, applies and bends down his mind, acquiring knowledge of
his former birds. In our births, ways he recalls his former birds, one bird, two birds, three, four,
five, ten, twenty, thirty, four, two, fifteen, hundred, a thousand, a hundred, a thousand birds,
the various destructions of aions, full cycles, the various renewal of aions, both the destruction
and renewal of aions, thus, I lived there, was named thus, was of such a plan of such a
cause, was thus supported, had such and such pleasant and painful experiences, had such
length of days, disappeared dense and arose elsewhere, there too I lived, was named, so and so,
of such a plan of such a cause, et cetera, thus he calls to mind in all that specific details,
in all their characteristics, in many various ways, his previous states of existence, this is the
first knowledge he attained, ignorance has vanished, knowledge arises, gone is the darkness,
a reason is the light, as it does for one who abides earnest, ardent and composed.
He then, with mind thus composed, made pure and translucent, bends down his mind to acquire knowledge
of the rise of the fall and rise of beings from one existence to another, the deva sight,
purified, and surpassing that of men, he behold beings, diseaseing and rising up again,
beings both mean and excellent, fair and far, gone to a happy state, gone to a warful state,
according to their deeds, so to say, Alas, these were these given to the practice of evil deeds,
of evil words, of evil thoughts, scoffing at the audience, of purverted views and reaping the
fruits of their purverted views, these beings on the disillusion of body, after death,
arose in the ways the warful planes, the downfall in hell, for he might think, ah,
these were these given to the practice of good deeds, of good words, of good thoughts,
not scoffing at the audience, but of sound views and reaping the fruits of their sound views,
these beings on the disillusion of the body, after death, arose again in the happy lot in the
heaven world. Thus with deva sight, purified and surpassing that of men, he behold beings,
diseaseing and rising up again, this is the second knowledge he attained, ignorance has vanished,
knowledge arises, gone is the darkness, the reason is the light as it does for one who abides
earnest, ardent and composed. He then with mind thus composed made pure and translucent, etc,
bends down his mind to acquire knowledge of the destruction of the Arsavas, he recognizes as it
really is the truth and this is Dukha, this is the arising of Dukha, this is the ending of Dukha,
this is the practice of that leads to the ending of Dukha. Here he recognizes the practice
that leads to the destruction of the Arsavas. In him thus knowing thus seeing, this mind is released
from the Arsava of sensuality, this mind is released from the Arsava of becoming from the
Arsava of ignorance, by release comes the knowledge that he is released so that he understands,
destroyed as rebirth, lived as the holy light, done as what I had to do, there is no more
coming to any state of being, this is the third knowledge he attained, ignorance has vanished,
knowledge arises, gone is the darkness, a reason is the light, as it does for one who abides earnest,
ardent and composed. That Brahmin is how one possesses the three full knowledge in the discipline
of the Arin and the Brahmin said, indeed master Gautama, this one is quite different from the other,
why master Gautama, he who has the three full knowledge of the Brahmin's is not worth
one sixteen part of him who has it in the discipline of the Arin, excellent it is master Gautama,
excellent, made a worthy Gautama, except me as a lay follower from this time for so long as life
lasts as one who has taken refuge in him. So in this Sutta, the Buddha is talking about the
three full knowledge that he himself attained, when he became a Buddha, we find in the Sutta,
then when the Buddha was enlightened, he attained this three full knowledge, but it is mentioned in the
Sutta that some of his disciples attained the six full knowledge, so it appears like at first the
Buddha attained the three full knowledge and later perhaps he developed another three,
that's why later also he had the six full knowledge, but he described himself as a person
having three full knowledge, which refers to the time when he was enlightened. And just now the last
part is talking about the destruction of the Arsabas. So this three full knowledge, the first one
is that he recalls the past lives. At the second one, he can see living beings being born and
reborn according to karma, so understands the working of karma. And the third one, he
contemplates the poor Arin fruits, the full noble fruits, and from there, it is destruction of the
Arsabas and became an Arahan, some are some Buddha. What is this Arsabas? Arsabas is this
probably translated as uncontrolled mental outflows. That means the running of the normal
six consciousness, when the six consciousness flows, then the world arises, because the Buddha said
that the whole world arises dependent on consciousness. That means the normal six full consciousness.
When the six full consciousness arises, the world arises and with it all the Dukka. So to get
out of Samsara, we have to destroy the Arsabas, so that these six consciousness stops working
and when the consciousness stops working, then Samsara will end.
